coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of FA/HA-functionalized carbon dots for human breast cancer cell targeted imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Tao, Junting
Zou, Hao
Liao, Xiaokun
Lu, Xinjian
cao, Jun
Pan, Jiaqi
Li, Chaorong
Zheng, Yingying
coredata.dc:description: Green fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were prepared by one-step hydrothermal method and then modified into folic acid functionalized carbon dots (FA-CDs) and hyaluronic acid functionalized carbon dots (HA-CDs) with targeted function to study their application in breast cancer cells imaging. The microstructure of the CDs observed through TEM showed the CDs with a scale of 2.69 nm. FT-IR and XPS showed the changes of bonds and functional groups that confirmed the transformation of COOH and NH2 to amide bonds. FA-CDs and HA-CDs had good water solubility and cytocompatibility, which laid a foundation for their application in human breast cancer cells imaging. At the same time, FA-CDs and HA-CDs had strong fluorescence excitation, and the optimal emission wavelength was about 450 nm. In fluorescence imaging of cells, carbon dots had bright green fluorescence in both breast cancer cells (MCF-7 cells) and normal cells (EC cells). After targeted endocytosis, FA-CDs and HA-CDs could emit bright green fluorescence in cancer cells but could not in normal cells, which proved that the synthesized FA-CDs and HA-CDs had targeting properties. FA-CDs and HA-CDs could be used to accurately identify breast cancer cells and normal cells as cancer diagnosis material, which had the potential application in early cancer diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon Dots
Green Fluorescent Carbon Dots
Photodiagnosis
hydrothermal method

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of folic acid or hyaluronic acid functionalized carbon dots (FA-CDs, HA-CDs)
Cytotoxicity assay of targeting carbon dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis diagram of FA-CDs and HA-CDs.
(a) TEM image, (b) HRTEM image and (c) size distribution of carbon dots (CDs).
FT-IR spectra of CDs, FA, FA-CDs, HA and HA-CDs.
Full XPS spectra of CDs, FA-CDs and HA-CDs.
XPS spectra of CDs, FA-CDs and HA-CDs; (a) C1s, O1s and N1s of CDs; (b) C1s, O1s and N1s of FA-CDs; (c) C1s, O1s and N1s of HA-CDs.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, FA-CDs and HA-CDs; Fluorescence emission spectra of (b) CDs, (c) FA-CDs and (d) HA-CDs.
The cell viability of MCF-7 cell after co-culture with different concentration (0, 50, 100, 150 and 200 μg/mL) of CDs, FA-CDs and HA-CDs for 24 h.
Fluorescence images of MCF-7 cells after co-cultured with (a) CDs, (b) FA-CDs and (c) HA-CDs.
Fluorescence images of EC cells after co-cultured with (a) CDs, (b) FA-CDs and (c) HA-CDs.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of FA/HA-functionalized carbon dots for human breast cancer cell targeted imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, and Key Laboratory of ATMMT Ministry of Education, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou 310018, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: and Key Laboratory of ATMMT Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, and Key Laboratory of ATMMT Ministry of Education, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou 310018, People's Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Green fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were prepared by one-step hydrothermal method and then modified into folic acid functionalized carbon dots (FA-CDs) and hyaluronic acid functionalized carbon dots (HA-CDs) with targeted function to study their application in breast cancer cells imaging. The microstructure of the CDs observed through TEM showed the CDs with a scale of 2.69 nm. FT-IR and XPS showed the changes of bonds and functional groups that confirmed the transformation of COOH and NH2 to amide bonds. FA-CDs and HA-CDs had good water solubility and cytocompatibility, which laid a foundation for their application in human breast cancer cells imaging. At the same time, FA-CDs and HA-CDs had strong fluorescence excitation, and the optimal emission wavelength was about 450 nm. In fluorescence imaging of cells, carbon dots had bright green fluorescence in both breast cancer cells (MCF-7 cells) and normal cells (EC cells). After targeted endocytosis, FA-CDs and HA-CDs could emit bright green fluorescence in cancer cells but could not in normal cells, which proved that the synthesized FA-CDs and HA-CDs had targeting properties. FA-CDs and HA-CDs could be used to accurately identify breast cancer cells and normal cells as cancer diagnosis material, which had the potential application in early cancer diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) is an ideal bioimaging materials [1], which have received special attention from researchers because of the advantages of photoluminescence, resistance to photobleaching, low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, and easy surface modification [2]. In contrast to conjugated polymers, carbon dots can be easy synthesized to tune their particle size to emit multicolor bright fluorescence. Meanwhile, compared with organic dyes [3–7] and semiconductor quantum dots [8,9], carbon dots have low cytotoxicity, excellent biocompatibility, and chemical inertness, which make them show more significant advantages in bioimaging applications, drug delivery, biosensing and others [10,11]. In this paper, with good inherent biocompatibility, water soluble nigrosine and betaine hydrochloride which are mainly compose of three elements of C, N and O, were used as a nontoxic excellent raw material for the synthesis of carbon dots. Meanwhile, the one-step hydrothermal method of CDs had the advantages of low fabrication cost and simplicity.

Breast cancer is a dangerous disease, but its early symptoms are inconspicuous. People often find the disease at an advanced stage when surgery is impossible [12], resulting in higher mortality at late diagnosis [13,14]. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop a rapid and early accurate cancer diagnosis method. The cell membrane is a biological structure composed of phospholipid bilayers and embedded proteins, in which some embedded special proteins can be used as biomarkers to distinguish cancer cells from normal cells [15,16]. Hence, tumor biomarkers are increasingly being used for fluorescent targeted imaging of cancer [17]. Folate receptor (FR) is one of the important tumor biomarkers, which is highly expressed in breast cancer cells membrane, but rare in normal cells membrane [18]. Differences in FR expression levels make it possible to detect cancers [19,20]. Folic acid (FA) is a water-soluble B vitamin that affects many functions of cells, including cell division, cell growth and immortalization. Folic acid has a high specific affinity and is an ideal ligand for FR. Therefore, coupling FA with fluorescent nanomaterials to fabricate targeted fluorescent materials is expected to be used to bond selectively to FRs to differentiate breast cancer cells [21–23].

CD44 is a cell membrane glycoprotein and surface receptor of cancer cells membrane, and its overexpression is closely related to the generation of cancer cells and the progression of tumors [24]. Hyaluronic acid (HA) is a naturally occurring acidic mucopolysaccharide composed of D-glucuronic acid and N-acetylglucosamine, which has the advantages of good biocompatibility, good biodegradability and non-toxicity [25,26]. In particular, HA has a specific affinity for the CD44 receptor and can bind to the targeting ligand CD44 [27], thereby identifying and distinguishing normal cells and cancer cells by judging the difference in the expression of CD44 on the surface of cancer cells. In recent years, HA surface modification and HA targeted drugs have been extensively studied, demonstrating that HA can be a useful functionalizing agent for CD44 receptors for targeted cancer cell imaging [28,29]. Zhang et al.[30] synthesized a multifunctional hyaluronic acid-functionalized carbon dots (HA/PEI-CDs) through a bottom-up approach. The prepared carbon dots had stable photoluminescence, excellent water solubility and biocompatibility. The findings showed that HA/PEI-CDs were easily taken up and functioned by cancer cells, and were suitable for real-time cell imaging and labeling.

In this paper, green fluorescent carbon dots were prepared by a hydrothermal method using water soluble nigrosine and betaine hydrochloride as raw materail. Then, CDs were combined with folic acid or hyaluronic acid to get functionalized carbon dots through the amidation reaction (see Scheme 1 ). Cell imaging was performed to study the recognition ability of CDs, FA-CDs and HA-CDs on breast cancer cells and normal cells. The prepared folic acid functionalized carbon dots (FA-CDs) and hyaluronic acid functionalized carbon dots (HA-CDs) successfully achieved targeting properties, showing bright green fluorescence in the imaging of cancer cells, but does not glow in the imaging of normal cells, showing the ability to distinguish the cancer cells and the normal cells.

Water soluble nigrosine, betaine hydrochloride, folic acid and hyaluronic acid were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N'-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride and N-hydroxysuccinimide purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Fetal bovine serum (FBS), dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM), phosphate buffered saline (PBS), trypsin cell digestion solution (containing phenol red), MTT cell proliferation and cytotoxicity assay kit and 4% paraformaldehyde were purchased from Beijing Labgic Technology Co., Ltd.

The microstructure and surface morphology of CDs were characterized by 200 kv transmission electron microscopy (JEOL JEM-2100, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) could be used to determine the element, content and bond type of the samples. In this experiment, a Kratos Axis Ultra device (Thermo Fisher, USA) with a 300 W Al-Kα source was used for the test. The Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FT-IR) of the samples were acquired by Nicolet 5700 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Thermo Electron, USA). The powdered potassium bromide tablet method was used for the collection, the potassium bromide background was subtracted, and the data collection range was 500-4000 cm−1. The ultraviolet-visible spectra (UV-Vis) of samples were acquired through a U-3900 UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The wavelength scanning mode was used for the test, the scanning speed was 600 nm/min, the scanning interval was 0.5 nm, and the slit width was 2 nm. The fluorescence spectra were characterized by F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer from Hitachi, Japan. The wavelength scanning mode was used during the fluorescence emission test, and the scanning speed was 240 nm/min. The absolute fluorescence QY was measured with a calibrated integrating sphere on the C-9920-02 quantum efficiency measurement system. The ELx 800 microplate reader (Berton Instruments, USA) was used to collect the absorbance values of the processed samples. Fluorescence images were taken with a confocal laser scanning microscope FV1200-IX81 (Olympus, Japan) and the selected excitation channel was 488 nm.

Green fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were prepared by one-step hydrothermal synthesis using nigrosine water soluble and betaine hydrochloride as raw materials. 0.25 g nigrosine was dissolved in 25 ml ultrapure water and was stirred magnetically until completely dissolved. 0.5 g betaine hydrochloride was slowly added and stirred well. The evenly mixed solution was transferred to the liner of 50 mL polyphenylene (PPL) hydrothermal synthesis reactor, sealed completely with stainless steel jacket, and placed in an oven at 250°C for 7 h of hydrothermal synthesis. The supernatant was filtered with slow speed filter paper and 0.22 μm filter head to collect the liquid. The collected liquid was used as the inner liquid of the dialysis bag, and the deionized water was used as the outer liquid of the dialysis bag. After dialysis for 24 h (MWCO = 1000 D), the liquid in the dialysis bag was freeze-dried. Finally, powder samples were collected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of folic acid or hyaluronic acid functionalized carbon dots (FA-CDs, HA-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared green fluorescent carbon dots powder was dissolved in deionized water and the concentration of carbon dots solution was 1.86 mg/mL.

30 mL PBS solution was placed in 100 mL conical flask and 40 mg folic acid was added into it. After folic acid was completely dissolved by fully stirring, 0.9 mmol EDC and 0.9 mmol NHS were added to folic acid solution under shading condition, and stirred slowly for 12 h. Then 5 ml of 1.86 mg/mL carbon dots solution was added to the 5 mL of the EDC and NHS mixture solutions, and the photophobic reaction was lasted for 24 h. After the reaction finished, the mixed solution was filtered by a 0.22 μm filter and the liquid was collected to dialyze by dialysis bag (MWCO = 1000 D) for 24 h. Then, the inner liquid of the dialysis bag was freeze-dried to obtain FA-CDs powder.

The preparation of hyaluronic acid functionalized carbon dots was similar to that of folic acid functionalized carbon dots. 23 mg of hyaluronic acid was first dissolved in 30 mL PBS solution. After being completely dissolved, 1 mmol EDC and 1 mmol NHS were added under shading condition and stirred for 24 h. Then 5 ml of 1.86 mg/mL carbon dots solution was added to the 5 mL of the mixture solutions in shading reaction for 36 h. After the reaction end, the mixed solution was filtered by a 0.22 μm filter and the liquid was collected to dialyze by dialysis bag (MWCO = 1000 D) for 24 h. Then, the inner liquid of the dialysis bag was collected and freeze-dried to obtain HA-CDS powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity assay of targeting carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Cytotoxicity of CDs, FA-CDs and HA-CDs was measured using CCK-8. Human breast cancer cells (MCF-7) were continuously cultured in high glucose medium containing 10% FBS and 1% double antibody. MCF-7 cells were resuspended first, and the total number of cells in resuspended solution was counted by blood counting plate. Then the cell density of the suspension was adjusted to 5000 cells /100 μL, and 100 μL cell suspension was added to each well of the 96-well plate, and incubated overnight in an incubator with 5% CO2 concentration at 37°C. According to the concentration gradient, FA-CDs solution with different concentrations (0, 50, 100, 150 and 200 μg/mL, respectively) was added to each well and incubated for 12 h in an incubator with 5% CO2 concentration at 37°C. Then 10 μL CCK-8 solutions were added to each well in the 96-well plate and incubate for 3 h. Finally, the absorbance value of sample solution per well at 490 nm was measured by ELx 800 microplate analyzer, and the values without cell pores were used as blank control.
The same method was used to determine the cytotoxicity of CDs and hyaluronic acid functionalized carbon dots (HA-CDs).

In vitro cell imaging of CDs, FA-CDs and HA-CDs was performed by confocal fluorescence imaging under laser confocal scanning microscope. Human breast cancer cells with overexpression of folate receptor and CD44 and normal EC cells were selected to study the bioimaging and recognition properties of functionalized carbon dots. Before cell imaging, cells were cultured in a high glucose medium containing 10% FBS and 1% double antibody and inoculated into laser confocal dishes at a density of 1–105 cells. After incubating for 12 h in an incubator with 5% CO2 concentration at 37°C, the original old culture medium was sucked out, and 2 mL high-sugar culture medium and 150 µL samples (CDs, FA-CDs and HA-CDs, respectively) were added to each confocal dish, and incubated together in an incubator with 5% CO2 concentration at 37°C for a period of time. After staining, the old medium containing functionalized carbon dots was sucked out and discarded, and washed gently with 1 mL PBS solution for 3 times. 100 µL 4% paraformaldehyde fixative was added to fix the cells for 10 min, and then the cells were gently rinsed with 1 mL PBS solution for 3 times. After 1 mL PBS solution was added to the confocal dish, fluorescence images were taken by laser confocal scanning microscope under excitation of 488 nm.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) can directly reflect the microstructure and size of nanomaterials. The carbon dots were analyzed using a 200 kV transmission electron microscope (shown in Fig. 1 ). The results of the Fig. 1(a) showed that the prepared carbon dots were quasi-spherical with uniform distribution and no obvious aggregation. Fig. 1(b) was a high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) image of the carbon dots, showing that the carbon dots had distinct lattice fringes with lattice spacing of 0.288 nm. TEM results also showed that CDs had heterogeneous crystal structure. Fig. 1(c) was the particle size distribution of carbon dots obtained by statistical analysis of the particles in Fig. 1(a) by Image J software. It showed that the average size of carbon dots was about 2.69 nm. This ultra-small particle size allowed the carbon dots to cross the cell membrane and enter the cell, where they could be photographed by fluorescence. The aqueous solution of CDs did not precipitate or aggregate for 1 months at room temperature. It showed that CDs had excellent water solubility and stability.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were used to characterize the chemical structure and surface functional groups of targeted functionalized carbons. Fig. 2 were the FT-IR spectra of CDs, FA, FA-CDs, HA and HA-CDs respectively. As shown in the FT-IR spectrum of synthesized CDs, the absorption peaks at 3450 cm−1 and 1712 cm−1 could be attributed to the stretching vibration of N-H and C=O, respectively, which indicated that there were a large number of the active groups on the surface of CDs, such as the amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups, which endowed CDs with superior hydrophility and dispersibility in water. The carboxyl group on folic acid reacted with the primary amine group on the carbon dots, which formed an amide bond that held this two together. In the FT-IR spectrum of FA-CDs, the bands of 3450 cm−1 and 1740 cm−1 were also assigned to the stretching vibrations of N-H and C=O, respectively. Compared to that in CDs, the absorption peak belonging to the C=O stretching vibration in FA-CDs was significantly shifted to high frequency. Meanwhile, In the FT-IR spectrum of HA, the absorption peaks at 3450 cm−1 and 1640 cm−1 were attributed to the O-H stretching vibration and the C=O stretching vibration, which directly proved the existence of COOH in HA. HA-CDs were formed after amidation reaction between CDs and HA. FT-IR spectrum of HA-CDs showed a new small peak at 1405 cm−1 indicating the presence of amide group (-NHCO-), which proved that HA and CDs was successfully recombined.

In order to more accurately judge the element content and surface chemical structure of FA-CDs and HA-CDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was used to analyze them. As could be seen from the full spectrum of XPS in Fig. 3 , there were three peaks separately at 285 eV, 399 eV and 532 eV, which illustrated that our as-prepared CDs, FA-CDs and HA-CDs were truly composed of three elements including carbon, nitrogen and oxygen. Fig. 4 (a), (b) and (c) were XPS high-resolution spectra of CDs, FA-CDs and HA-CDs respectively. In the high resolution spectra of CDs (shown in Fig. 4 (a)), the C1s could further be divided into four C-C, C-N, C-O and O-C=O bonds at bond energies of 284.67 eV, 285.28 eV, 286.06 eV and 286.91 eV. Similarly, N1s could also be divided into 399.40 eV and 401.53 eV, corresponding to pyridine nitrogen (C-N) and amino nitrogen (NH2), respectively. O1s could also be divided into 531.21 eV and 532.33 eV, corresponding to C-O and C=O, respectively. All the results above suggested that CDs had a large number of carboxylic acid groups and primary amino groups, and had good hydrophilicity.

In the C1s high resolution spectra of CDs, FA-CDs and HA-CDs, the binding energy peak of O-C=O was located differently at 286.91 eV, 288.51 eV and 287.48 eV, respectively, and the percentage of O-C=O bond peak area in C1s increased from 5.42% to 18.35% and 18.65%, respectively. The binding energy peak of C-O was located slightly differently at 286.06 eV, 286.09 eV and 285.59 eV for CDs, FA-CDs and HA-CDs, respectively, and the percentage of C-O bond peak area in C1s increased from 13.25% to 26.9% and 27.46%. The binding energy peak of C-N was located at 285.28 eV, 285.59 eV and 286.09 eV for CDs, FA-CDs and HA-CDs respectively, and the percentage of C-N bond peak area increased from 21.08% to 26.09% and 27.46%. All the increase of O-C=O, C-O and C-N content was due to the connection of CDs with FA or HA. During the amide reaction of FA or HA with CDs, O=C-O converted to O=C-N. In the high resolution spectra of O1s, the binding energy peak of C=O for CDs, FA-CDs and HA-CDs were located slightly differently at 532.33 eV, 532.77 eV and 532.08 eV, respectively, and the proportion of C=O bond peak area in O1s decreased from 59.52% in CDs to 54.64% in FA-CDs and 54.35% in HA-CDs, which proved the generation of amide groups. In the N1s high resolution spectra of CDs, FA-CDS and HA-CDS, the binding energy peak of C-N was located slightly differently at 399.53 eV, 399.93 eV and 399.74 eV respectively, and the percentages of C-N increased, which also proved the synthesis of amide bonds.

Ultraviolet-visible (UV) and fluorescence spectra (PL) could be used to characterize the optical properties of FA-CDs and HA-CDs. The absorption peak of CDs (Fig. 5 (a)) at 260 nm could be attributed to the π-π* electronic transition of the aromatic sp2 domain and absorption peak at 358 nm might be due to the n-π* transition of C=N and C=O bonds. The broad absorption peak of FA-CDs at 250 nm might also be ascribed to the aromatic C=C bond π-π* electronic transition. FA-CDs also had a wide peak at 355 nm, which was usually assigned to the n-π* transition of surface group or molecule connection at the carbon core edge [31]. HA-CDs had a narrow front at 259 nm, which could be attributed to the aromatic C=C bond π-π* transition. Compared with CDs, the conjugation degree was decreased after the CDs was combined with FA or HA, resulting in a slight blue shift of the absorption peaks of FA-CDs and HA-CDs. Fig. 5 (b, c and d) were fluorescence emission spectra of CDs, FA-CDs and HA-CDs, respectively. Fig. 5(b) showed that CDs had good fluorescence performance. With the excitation wavelength changed, its emission peak kept invariant, and the maximum excitation wavelength was 350 nm, and the corresponding stronger emission wavelength was 480 nm. Fig. 5(c) was the emission spectra of FA-CDs, which showed that FA-CDs had a wide emission wavelength. There was no excitation dependence. The emission peak did not shift with the change of excitation wavelength. The maximum fluorescence emission was achieved under 390 nm excitation, and the emission wavelength was 452 nm, which was different from that of pure carbon dots. Compared with CDs, FA-CDs had less conjugated structures, which reduced the energy and made the structure more stable. In Fig. 5(d), when the excitation wavelength was increased from 240 to 260 nm, HA-CDs emission peak had a slight red-shift with the increase of excitation wavelength. The slight red-shift of HA-CDs emission was due to steric hindrance effect after introducing chromophore by amide reaction. Finally, HA-CDs got the strongest fluorescence emission at 450 nm when the maximum excitation wavelength was 260 nm. It had been measured the absolute quantum yield of CDs in aqueous solution was 15.5%, which was higher than previously reported [32].

The cytotoxicity of materials seriously restricts their application in biological imaging. Low toxicity and good biocompatibility are important indexes to evaluate the properties of biomaterials. In this paper, CCK-8 assay was used to detect the cytotoxicity of carbon dots (CDs), folic acid functionalized carbon dots (FA-CDs) and hyaluronic acid functionalized carbon dots (HA-CDs) in MCF-7 cells. As shown in Fig. 6 , CDs, FA-CDs and HA-CDs were cultured according to the standard CCK-8 method with concentration gradients of 0, 50, 100, 150 and 200 μg/mL. As could be seen from Fig. 6, after MCF-7 cells was co-cultured with the material for 24 h, the cell viability value remained above 80%, showing relatively low cytotoxicity. Even when the concentration of the material was raised to 200 μg/mL, the cell viability value of FA-CDs reached 85%, showing good biocompatibility. According to CCK-8 test results, it could be concluded that FA-CDs and HA-CDs had very low cytotoxicity and excellent cytocompatibility, which could be applied in biomedical imaging.

FA-CDs and HA-CDs with targeting properties were synthesized by combining carbon dots with targeting groups, which were specific fluorescent nanomaterials targeting breast cancer cells. In order to verify this specificity, FA-CDs and HA-CDs were co-cultured with cells and fluorescent photographs were taken by laser confocal scanning microscope (LCSM). Human breast cancer cells (MCF-7) and normal epithelial cells (EC) were used to imaging as comparison. CDs, FA-CDs and HA-CDs were cultured with MCF-7 cells respectively in an incubator with 5% CO2 concentration at 37°C for 4 h. After the dishes were processed, fluorescence photos were taken under the 488 nm channel by laser confocal scanning microscope. Fig. 7 (a, b and c) were fluorescence images of CDs, FA-CDs and HA-CDs after co-cultured with MCF-7 cells respectively. It could be seen from the figures that all the three materials entered into cells and emitted green fluorescence under excitation of the 488 nm laser, proving that all the three materials had the ability of cell imaging. Fluorescence images associated with light field showed that FA-CDs and HA-CDs existed in the cytoplasmic region around the nucleus, and there was almost no luminescence in the nucleus. This might be due to the fact that FA-CDs and HA-CDs were absorbed by MCF-7 cells and diffused into the cytoplasm, which was mediated by FR and CD44 mediated endocytosis and could not enter the nucleus. Therefore, FA-CDs and HA-CDs with high fluorescence and cell internalization properties could be applied to tumor-targeted cell imaging. After co-culture with MCF-7 cells, FA-CDs had brighter green fluorescence than HA-CDs at the same test conditions, which indicated that the FA-CDs had better specificity than HA-CDs. This might be due to the fact that FR was more sensitive than CD44 on the membrane of cancer cells. The result showed that FA-CDs could be better applied in human breast cancer cells imaging.

In order to verify the targeting performance of FA-CDs and HA-CDs in breast cancer cell recognition, CDs, FA-CDs and HA-CDs were co-cultured with EC cells respectively for fluorescence imaging. Carbon dots were a good cell imaging material, with a very small size, free access to cells, and had been widely used for biological imaging [33]. Fig. 8 (a) was a fluorescent image of EC cells cultured with carbon dots, which showed the carbon dots entered the cells and glowed green in the cytoplasm. As shown in Fig. 8(b) and (c), fluorescence images of EC cells co-cultured with FA-CDs and HA-CDs respectively had no green fluorescence, which proved that FA-CDs and HA-CDs did not enter EC cells. FR and CD44 did not be overexpressed on the surface of EC cells membranes and therefore EC cells could not recognize folic acid and hyaluronic acid group bond to FA-CDs and HA-CDs respectively. FA-CDs and HA-CDs could enter cancer cells MCF-7 and fluoresce in cytoplasm, while FA-CDs and HA-CDs could not enter normal EC cells, which demonstrated the specificity of the FA-CDs or HA-CDs. The prepared FA-CDs and HA-CDS were excellent targeted biological imaging materials and could be used to distinguish cancer cells from normal cells.

In summary, green fluorescent CDs was successfully synthesized by hydrothermal method using natural water soluble nigrosine and betaine hydrochloride as excellent nontoxic raw material firstly. Folic acid (FA) and hyaluronic acid (HA) were then combined with CDs by amide bond to form FA-CDs and HA-CDs, respectively. FA-CDs and HA-CDs had good water solubility, cytocompatibility, excellent fluorescence properties and targeted recognition of overexpressed folate receptor or CD44 on cancer cell. Laser confocal microscopy imaging showed that human breast cancer cells (MCF-7) could emit green fluorescence after co-cultured with FA-CDs and HA-CDs, while normal cells (EC cells) could not under the same experimental condition, which proved that the FA-CDs and HA-CDs had targeting properties to accurately identify human breast cancer cells and EC cells. MCF-7 co-cultured with FA-CDs emitted brighter fluorescence than that co-cultured with HA-CDs because the targeting of FA group was better than HA group. Therefore, FA-CDs could be used as excellent fluorescent material for human breast cancer cells imaging, which would be contributory to early diagnosis of cancer.

Junting Tao: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Hao Zou: Methodology, Investigation. Xiaokun Liao: Methodology, Investigation. Xinjian Lu: Methodology, Investigation. Jun cao: . Jiaqi Pan: Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Chaorong Li: Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yingying Zheng: .

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The author declare no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51802282) and the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No. LQ17F040004).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Valorisation of bio-derived fluorescent carbon dots for metal sensing, DNA binding and bioimaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Gracia K D J, Thavamani S S, Amaladhas T P, et al. Valorisation of bio-derived fluorescent carbon dots for metal sensing, DNA binding and bioimaging, J. Chemosphere, 2022, 298: 134128.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In vivo study of a novel, safe, rapid, and targeted red carbon dot probe for recognition of tumors with high expression of folate enzyme

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhong Z, Li X, Liu S, et al. In vivo study of a novel, safe, rapid, and targeted red carbon dot probe for recognition of tumors with high expression of folate enzyme, J. RSC Adv.2021, 11(46): 28809-28817.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Water-soluble polythiophene for two-photon excitation fluorescence imaging and photodynamic therapy of cancer

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Lan M, Zhao S, Xie Y, et al. Water-soluble polythiophene for two-photon excitation fluorescence imaging and photodynamic therapy of cancer, J. ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces, 2017, 9(17): 14590-14595.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: NIR emission nanoparticles based on FRET composed of AIE luminogens and NIR dyes for two-photon fluorescence imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Liu L J, Liu W, Ji G, et al. NIR emission nanoparticles based on FRET composed of AIE luminogens and NIR dyes for two-photon fluorescence imaging, J. Chin. J. Polym. Sci, 2019, 37(4): 401-408.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Diketopyrrolopyrrole-derived organic small molecular dyes for tumor phototheranostics
